questions.question: Maya has utility U(x,y)=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}, income m=120, price of coffee x is p_x=4, and price of sandwiches y is p_y=6. Using the Lagrangian method, what bundle maximizes utility and what is the economic interpretation of the first-order conditions?

choices.A: x*=15, y*=10; the consumer spends equal dollar amounts on both goods because the Cobb-Douglas exponents are equal.
choices.B: x*=20, y*=6.67; the consumer equates marginal utilities MU_x=MU_y.
choices.C: x*=30, y*=0; coffee is cheaper, so all income should be spent on coffee.
choices.D: x*=12, y*=12; equal quantities maximize utility because the exponents are equal.

questions.explanation: For U=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}, MRS=MU_x/MU_y=y/x. The FOC gives y/x=p_x/p_y=4/6, so y=(2/3)x. With 4x+6y=120, x=15 and y=10. B incorrectly equates marginal utilities instead of marginal utility per dollar. C is a wrong corner setup. D confuses equal exponents with equal quantities rather than equal expenditure shares.

questions.question: Noah has utility U(x,y)=x^{1/3}y^{2/3}, income m=180, p_x=3, and p_y=6. Which Lagrangian solution is correct, and what does it imply about spending on x and y?

choices.A: x*=30, y*=15; spending is equal across goods.
choices.B: x*=20, y*=20; quantities are equal because utility is multiplicative.
choices.C: x*=20, y*=20; spending on y is twice spending on x.
choices.D: x*=60, y*=0; y is too expensive, so the optimum is a corner.

questions.explanation: Cobb-Douglas demands are x*=a/(a+b)m/p_x=(1/3)(180)/3=20 and y*=b/(a+b)m/p_y=(2/3)(180)/6=20. Spending is 60 on x and 120 on y, so y receives twice the expenditure share. A uses equal spending. B has the right quantities but wrong interpretation. D wrongly treats a standard Cobb-Douglas problem as a corner solution.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}, income m=100, p_x=5, and p_y=5. If p_x rises to 10 while income and p_y stay fixed, what happens to x* and why?

choices.A: x* stays at 10 because the utility exponents are unchanged.
choices.B: x* falls from 10 to 5 because the fixed one-half expenditure share is now divided by a higher price of x.
choices.C: x* rises from 10 to 20 because a higher price makes x more valuable.
choices.D: x* falls from 10 to 0 because a price increase always creates a corner solution.

questions.explanation: For equal-exponent Cobb-Douglas, x*=m/(2p_x). Initially x=100/(25)=10; after p_x=10, x=100/(210)=5. The mechanism is the fixed expenditure share combined with a higher price per unit. A ignores price effects. C reverses the law of demand. D incorrectly assumes any price increase causes a corner.

questions.question: Lena maximizes U(x,y)=x^{1/4}y^{3/4}. Initially m=160, p_x=4, p_y=8. Her income rises to m=240 with prices unchanged. What happens to her demand for y, and what is the mechanism?

choices.A: y* rises from 15 to 22.5 because y keeps a constant 3/4 income share while income rises.
choices.B: y* rises from 20 to 30 because y keeps a constant 1/2 income share.
choices.C: y* stays at 15 because Cobb-Douglas demands depend only on prices.
choices.D: y* falls from 15 to 10 because higher income reduces marginal utility.

questions.explanation: y*=3/4m/p_y. Initially y=(3/4)(160)/8=15; after income rises, y=(3/4)(240)/8=22.5. The mechanism is constant expenditure shares in Cobb-Douglas preferences. B uses the wrong expenditure share. C describes quasilinear-style demand, not Cobb-Douglas. D confuses diminishing marginal utility with a negative income effect.

questions.question: A consumer has smooth, strictly convex indifference curves over burritos x and smoothies y. The budget line is 2x+4y=40. At bundle (8,6), the indifference curve has slope -1 while the budget line has slope -1/2. Which statement best describes the graph and the direction of utility improvement?

choices.A: The consumer is at a tangency because both slopes are negative.
choices.B: The consumer should move toward more smoothies and fewer burritos because the indifference curve is steeper than the budget line.
choices.C: The consumer should move toward more burritos and fewer smoothies because the MRS exceeds the market trade-off.
choices.D: The consumer should stay at (8,6) because it lies on the budget line.

questions.explanation: The budget slope is -p_x/p_y=-2/4=-1/2. The indifference curve slope is -MRS=-1, so MRS=1 exceeds p_x/p_y=1/2. The consumer values burritos more than the market requires, so moving toward more x and less y raises utility. A ignores slope magnitudes. B reverses the direction. D ignores that affordable bundles on the budget line need not be optimal.

questions.question: A budget line for tickets x and snacks y has intercepts x=12 and y=8. At a proposed interior optimum, the indifference curve through the bundle is flatter than the budget line. What does this imply?

choices.A: The proposed bundle is optimal because any intersection with an indifference curve is optimal.
choices.B: The consumer should choose more x and less y because the market gives up too little y per x.
choices.C: The consumer should choose less x and more y because MRS is below p_x/p_y.
choices.D: The budget line must be tangent because the intercepts are positive.

questions.explanation: If the indifference curve is flatter than the budget line, then MRS<p_x/p_y. The consumer is willing to give up less y for x than the market requires, so she should move toward less x and more y. A treats any feasible bundle as optimal. B reverses the implication. D confuses positive intercepts with tangency.

questions.question: In the Lagrangian for maximizing U(x,y)=ln x+ln y subject to 5x+10y=100, the multiplier at the optimum is lambda=0.02. What is the best economic interpretation of lambda?

choices.A: It is the ratio of prices, p_x/p_y.
choices.B: It is the marginal utility of x at the optimum.
choices.C: It is the approximate increase in maximized utility from one more dollar of income.
choices.D: It is the quantity of y purchased per dollar.

questions.explanation: The Lagrange multiplier on the budget constraint measures the marginal utility of income: the increase in indirect utility from relaxing the budget by one dollar. A confuses lambda with the price ratio. B confuses lambda with MU_x; actually MU_x=lambda p_x. D gives no correct interpretation of the multiplier.

questions.question: A meal-kit company wants to predict how a student allocates a $90 weekly food budget between frozen meals x and restaurant meals y. The student has U(x,y)=x^{2/3}y^{1/3}, frozen meals cost $5, and restaurant meals cost $15. Which model should be used and what prediction follows?

choices.A: Use utility maximization with a budget constraint; x=12 and y*=2 because the student spends two-thirds of income on frozen meals and one-third on restaurant meals.
choices.B: Use profit maximization; x*=18 and y*=0 because frozen meals have the lower cost.
choices.C: Use utility maximization without a budget constraint; choose infinite quantities because utility is increasing.
choices.D: Use expenditure minimization for a fixed utility level; x*=6 and y*=4 because quantities should sum to 10.

questions.explanation: The scenario asks for predicted consumption from income, prices, and preferences, so Marshallian utility maximization is the correct model. x=(2/3)(90)/5=12 and y=(1/3)(90)/15=2. B applies a producer model. C ignores scarcity. D uses the wrong dual problem and an arbitrary quantity sum.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes the budget constraint from the utility function in the consumer's optimization problem for books x and coffee y?

choices.A: The budget constraint describes preferences, while the utility function describes prices and income.
choices.B: The budget constraint gives feasible bundles from prices and income, while the utility function ranks those feasible bundles.
choices.C: Both the budget constraint and utility function rank bundles, but the budget constraint ranks them in dollars.
choices.D: The utility function determines which bundles are affordable, while the budget constraint determines marginal utility.

questions.explanation: The budget constraint defines the feasible set using prices and income; the utility function ranks bundles by preference. A reverses the roles. C incorrectly says the budget ranks bundles. D incorrectly assigns affordability to utility and marginal utility to the budget.

questions.question: Jamal's indirect utility from maximizing U(x,y)=x^{1/2}y^{1/2} with p_x=4, p_y=4, and income m is V=m/(2sqrt(p_xp_y)). Initially m=80. A scholarship increases income to 100. What is the change in maximized utility, and how should it be evaluated?

choices.A: Utility rises by 2.5, showing the welfare gain from relaxing the budget constraint.
choices.B: Utility rises by 20, because income rises by 20 dollars.
choices.C: Utility is unchanged because prices did not change.
choices.D: Utility falls by 2.5 because marginal utility diminishes.

questions.explanation: With p_x=p_y=4, V=m/(24)=m/8. Utility rises from 80/8=10 to 100/8=12.5, a gain of 2.5. B confuses dollars with utility. C ignores that higher income expands the budget set. D reverses the welfare effect of more income.

questions.question: A student has U(x,y)=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}, income m=96, p_x=4, and p_y=8. Assuming an interior solution, what bundle satisfies the tangency condition and the budget constraint?

choices.A: x=12, y*=6; MRS=p_x/p_y and all income is spent.
choices.B: x*=8, y*=8; equal utility exponents imply equal quantities.
choices.C: x*=24, y*=0; x is cheaper, so the optimum cannot be interior.
choices.D: x*=6, y*=12; the price ratio implies y/x=2.

questions.explanation: MRS=y/x. Tangency gives y/x=4/8=1/2, so y=x/2. Budget 4x+8y=96 gives x=12, y=6. B confuses equal exponents with equal quantities. C wrongly chooses a corner despite strictly convex monotonic preferences. D inverts the price ratio.

questions.question: Priya maximizes U(x,y)=x^{3/4}y^{1/4} with income m=200, p_x=10, and p_y=5. Which interior bundle is optimal, and what expenditure pattern does it imply?

choices.A: x*=10, y*=20; spending is equal because both goods are essential.
choices.B: x*=15, y*=10; three-fourths of income is spent on x and one-fourth on y.
choices.C: x*=20, y*=0; the higher exponent on x implies a corner on x.
choices.D: x*=5, y*=30; one-fourth of income is spent on x.

questions.explanation: Cobb-Douglas demands give x=(3/4)(200)/10=15 and y=(1/4)(200)/5=10. Spending is 150 on x and 50 on y. A uses equal spending incorrectly. C falsely treats a high exponent as a corner. D reverses the expenditure shares.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}, m=120, p_x=4, p_y=6. Suppose p_y falls to 3. What happens to the interior optimum and why?

choices.A: x* falls from 15 to 7.5 because y became cheaper.
choices.B: y* rises from 10 to 20 because the fixed one-half expenditure share buys more y at the lower price.
choices.C: y* stays at 10 because interior solutions always keep quantities fixed.
choices.D: x* rises from 15 to 30 because the consumer substitutes toward x.

questions.explanation: With equal Cobb-Douglas exponents, y*=m/(2p_y). Initially y=120/(12)=10; after p_y=3, y=120/6=20. The mechanism is a lower own price increasing quantity purchased for the same expenditure share. A reports the change in x incorrectly; x remains 15. C ignores price effects. D reverses substitution toward the cheaper good.

questions.question: A consumer with smooth strictly convex preferences has an interior optimum for apples x and bananas y. At current prices p_x=2 and p_y=4, MRS=1/2 at the optimum. If p_x rises to 4 while p_y and income stay fixed, what must happen at the new interior optimum?

choices.A: MRS must rise to 1 because the market trade-off p_x/p_y rises; the consumer moves to a bundle where apples are relatively scarcer.
choices.B: MRS must fall to 1/4 because apples are now more expensive.
choices.C: MRS stays at 1/2 because preferences do not change.
choices.D: The consumer must buy only bananas because any price increase rules out an interior optimum.

questions.explanation: Interior optimality requires MRS=p_x/p_y. The ratio rises from 2/4=1/2 to 4/4=1. With convex preferences, this means moving to a bundle where x is lower relative to y, raising MRS. B inverts the price ratio. C ignores that the chosen bundle changes. D incorrectly turns a possible interior solution into a necessary corner.

questions.question: A consumer's indifference curves for tea x and cookies y are smooth and strictly convex. The budget line has slope -2. At the chosen bundle, the indifference curve is tangent to the budget line in the positive quadrant. What must be true?

choices.A: The MRS equals 2, and the bundle has positive quantities of both goods.
choices.B: The MRS equals -2, and utility is minimized.
choices.C: The price of tea must equal the price of cookies.
choices.D: The consumer must be at a corner because the budget line is downward sloping.

questions.explanation: The indifference curve slope is -MRS, so tangency to a budget line with slope -2 implies MRS=2. Since the tangency occurs in the positive quadrant, both goods are consumed. B gives the slope rather than the positive MRS and says minimization. C assumes equal prices despite slope -2. D ignores the stated interior tangency.

questions.question: For concert tickets x and parking passes y, a consumer has monotonic, convex preferences. Her budget line shifts outward parallel after income rises. If the original optimum and new optimum are both interior, which graphical feature remains unchanged?

choices.A: The intercepts remain unchanged.
choices.B: The slope of the budget line remains unchanged, so the tangency condition uses the same price ratio.
choices.C: The chosen quantities remain unchanged.
choices.D: The indifference curve through the optimum remains the same.

questions.explanation: A parallel outward budget shift changes intercepts but keeps slope -p_x/p_y unchanged. At an interior optimum, MRS still equals the same price ratio. A is wrong because income changes intercepts. C is wrong because higher income changes the chosen bundle for normal goods. D is wrong because the consumer reaches a higher indifference curve.

questions.question: At an interior optimum for U(x,y), the condition MU_x/p_x=MU_y/p_y holds. What does this mean economically?

choices.A: The consumer receives the same total utility from x and y.
choices.B: The consumer spends the same number of dollars on each good.
choices.C: The last dollar spent on each good yields the same marginal utility, so no budget reallocation can raise utility.
choices.D: The marginal utilities of the two goods must be numerically equal.

questions.explanation: Interior optimality equalizes marginal utility per dollar across goods. If one were higher, shifting a dollar toward that good would raise utility. A confuses marginal and total utility. B is true only for special utility forms. D ignores prices.

questions.question: A researcher observes that Emma buys positive amounts of both streaming subscriptions x and gym visits y. Prices are p_x=12 and p_y=6, and her preferences are smooth, monotonic, and strictly convex. Which model condition should the researcher test at Emma's optimum?

choices.A: MU_x=MU_y because both goods are consumed.
choices.B: MRS=p_x/p_y=2 because the solution is interior.
choices.C: MRS=p_y/p_x=1/2 because y is cheaper.
choices.D: x=0 or y=0 because strict convexity implies a corner.

questions.explanation: Positive consumption of both goods with smooth convex preferences indicates an interior solution, so the tangency condition is MRS=p_x/p_y=12/6=2. A omits prices. C inverts the price ratio. D reverses the implication of strict convexity.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes an interior solution from a corner solution in a two-good consumer problem?

choices.A: An interior solution has positive quantities of both goods and usually satisfies tangency; a corner solution has zero consumption of at least one good and may not satisfy tangency.
choices.B: An interior solution means the consumer spends less than income; a corner solution means the consumer spends all income.
choices.C: An interior solution occurs only when prices are equal; a corner solution occurs only when prices differ.
choices.D: An interior solution maximizes utility; a corner solution does not maximize utility.

questions.explanation: Interior solutions involve strictly positive quantities and are characterized by MRS=p_x/p_y when differentiable. Corner solutions are still utility-maximizing but occur at the boundary. B is wrong because monotonic preferences imply full spending in both cases. C invents a price-equality requirement. D wrongly says corners are not optima.

questions.question: Sara has U(x,y)=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}. Initially p_x=5, p_y=5, and m=100. A campus grant raises income to 120 with prices unchanged. Using the maximized utility value from the optimal interior bundle, what is the welfare gain?

choices.A: 0, because prices are unchanged.
choices.B: 2, because the optimal bundle changes from (10,10) to (12,12), raising utility from 10 to 12.
choices.C: 20, because income rises by 20 dollars.
choices.D: 4, because both goods rise by 2 units.

questions.explanation: Initially x=y=100/(25)=10 and U=10. After income rises, x=y=120/(25)=12 and U=12. Welfare measured by maximized utility rises by 2. A ignores income effects. C confuses dollars with utility. D adds quantity changes rather than computing utility.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=4ln x+2ln y, p_x=8, p_y=4, and m=120, which bundle satisfies the tangency condition and budget constraint?

choices.A: x*=10, y*=10; equal quantities satisfy tangency.
choices.B: x*=5, y*=20; y is cheaper, so more y is purchased.
choices.C: x*=10, y*=10; MRS=2y/x equals p_x/p_y=2.
choices.D: x*=15, y*=0; x has the larger utility weight.

questions.explanation: MU_x=4/x and MU_y=2/y, so MRS=(4/x)/(2/y)=2y/x. Tangency gives 2y/x=8/4=2, so y=x. Budget 8x+4y=120 gives x=y=10. A has the right bundle but not the correct reasoning. B follows cheapness alone. D incorrectly chooses a corner.

questions.question: A consumer maximizes U(x,y)=xy subject to 2x+8y=80. Using the tangency condition derived from the Lagrangian, what is the optimal bundle and its interpretation?

choices.A: x*=20, y*=5; the consumer sets MRS=y/x=1/4, matching the market trade-off.
choices.B: x*=10, y*=7.5; the consumer equalizes quantities weighted by prices.
choices.C: x*=40, y*=0; x is cheaper, so all income goes to x.
choices.D: x*=16, y*=6; the consumer spends 40 percent of income on x.

questions.explanation: For U=xy, MRS=y/x. Tangency gives y/x=p_x/p_y=2/8=1/4, so y=x/4. Budget gives 2x+8(x/4)=4x=80, so x=20 and y=5. B uses no valid tangency. C mistakes cheaper good for corner optimality. D uses an arbitrary spending share.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=xy, m=100, p_x=5, and p_y=5. If p_x rises to 10, what happens to the tangency condition and the optimal x?

choices.A: The tangency MRS falls from 1 to 1/2 and x rises from 10 to 20.
choices.B: The tangency MRS rises from 1 to 2 and x falls from 10 to 5 because the market requires more y per unit of x.
choices.C: The tangency MRS remains 1 and x remains 10 because preferences are unchanged.
choices.D: Tangency no longer applies because price changes create nonconvex preferences.

questions.explanation: At an interior optimum, MRS=p_x/p_y. The ratio rises from 1 to 2. Cobb-Douglas demand x=m/(2p_x) falls from 10 to 5. A inverts the ratio. C ignores the changed budget slope. D falsely links price changes to preference convexity.

questions.question: Tara has U(x,y)=x^{1/3}y^{2/3}, m=180, p_x=6, and p_y=6. If p_y rises to 12, what happens to y* and through what mechanism?

choices.A: y* rises from 20 to 40 because y has a larger utility exponent.
choices.B: y* stays at 20 because the exponent on y is unchanged.
choices.C: y* falls from 20 to 10 because the same two-thirds expenditure share buys fewer units of y at the higher price.
choices.D: y* falls from 10 to 5 because only one-third of income is spent on y.

questions.explanation: y*=(2/3)m/p_y. Initially y=(2/3)(180)/6=20; after p_y=12, y=10. The mechanism is a steeper budget trade-off and fixed Cobb-Douglas expenditure share. A reverses the price effect. B ignores price. D uses the wrong expenditure share and initial quantity.

questions.question: On a graph with x on the horizontal axis and y on the vertical axis, the budget line has slope -3. At bundle A, the indifference curve has slope -5. Which statement best interprets this failure of tangency?

choices.A: The consumer's MRS is 5, above the price ratio 3, so moving toward more x and less y can raise utility.
choices.B: The consumer's MRS is -5, below the price ratio -3, so the bundle is optimal.
choices.C: The consumer should move toward less x because the indifference curve is steeper than the budget line.
choices.D: Tangency holds because both curves slope downward.

questions.explanation: The indifference curve slope is -MRS, so MRS=5. The budget slope is -p_x/p_y=-3, so p_x/p_y=3. Since MRS>price ratio, the consumer values x relatively more than the market, so more x and less y improves utility. B mishandles signs. C reverses the direction. D ignores slope magnitudes.

questions.question: A consumer's budget line rotates inward around the y-intercept when the price of x rises. For an interior optimum with convex indifference curves, what graphical change must occur at the new tangency?

choices.A: The budget line becomes flatter, and the new tangency has a lower MRS.
choices.B: The budget line becomes steeper, and the new tangency has a higher MRS.
choices.C: The budget line shifts outward in parallel, and the same indifference curve is reached.
choices.D: The indifference curves become concave because prices changed.

questions.explanation: A rise in p_x makes the budget slope -p_x/p_y more negative, so the line becomes steeper. At an interior tangency, MRS rises to match the higher price ratio. A reverses the rotation. C describes an income increase, not an x-price increase. D wrongly says prices change preferences.

questions.question: At a bundle of pizza x and soda y, MRS=4 while p_x/p_y=2. What does this economically mean?

choices.A: The consumer is willing to give up 4 sodas for one pizza, while the market requires only 2 sodas, so pizza is relatively under-consumed at this bundle.
choices.B: The consumer is willing to give up 2 pizzas for one soda, so soda is under-consumed.
choices.C: The consumer is at the optimum because both numbers are positive.
choices.D: The consumer should buy no pizza because MRS exceeds the price ratio.

questions.explanation: MRS=4 means the consumer values one more pizza at 4 sodas, but the market price is only 2 sodas per pizza. She should move toward more pizza. B reverses the trade-off. C ignores equality. D reverses the implication.

questions.question: A tutoring app observes a student choosing between hours of tutoring x and hours of entertainment y. The student buys both goods, preferences are smooth and convex, tutoring costs $30 per hour, entertainment costs $10 per hour, and income is fully spent. Which condition should be used to test whether the observed bundle could be optimal?

choices.A: MRS=1/3 because tutoring is three times as expensive.
choices.B: MU_x=MU_y because both are measured in hours.
choices.C: MRS=3 because the market trades one tutoring hour for three entertainment hours.
choices.D: x=y because an interior optimum requires equal quantities.

questions.explanation: The relevant model is interior utility maximization, so MRS=p_x/p_y=30/10=3. A inverts the price ratio. B ignores prices. D confuses interiority with equal quantities.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes MRS from the price ratio p_x/p_y?

choices.A: MRS is determined by preferences at a bundle; p_x/p_y is determined by market prices and is the budget line's absolute slope.
choices.B: MRS is the market cost of x; p_x/p_y is the consumer's personal willingness to trade.
choices.C: MRS and p_x/p_y are always equal at every feasible bundle.
choices.D: MRS is measured in dollars; p_x/p_y is measured in utility.

questions.explanation: MRS is the preference trade-off, while p_x/p_y is the market trade-off. They are equal only at an interior optimum. B reverses the concepts. C overgeneralizes tangency. D gives incorrect units.

questions.question: Kai has U(x,y)=xy, m=80, and p_y=4. Initially p_x=4; then p_x falls to 2. Using the optimized bundles, what happens to maximized utility?

choices.A: It rises from 100 to 200 because x demand doubles while spending shares remain equal.
choices.B: It rises from 80 to 160 because the price of x is cut in half.
choices.C: It stays at 100 because income and p_y are unchanged.
choices.D: It falls from 200 to 100 because lower prices reduce marginal utility.

questions.explanation: For U=xy, x=m/(2p_x), y=m/(2p_y). Initially x=10, y=10, U=100. After p_x=2, x=20, y=10, U=200. B confuses income with utility. C ignores the price change. D reverses welfare effects.

questions.question: Alex has utility U(x,y)=3x+y, income m=60, p_x=5, and p_y=4. Solve the utility maximization problem and interpret why the optimum is a corner.

choices.A: x*=12, y*=0 because MU_x/p_x=3/5 exceeds MU_y/p_y=1/4, so every dollar gives more utility when spent on x.
choices.B: x*=0, y*=15 because y is cheaper in dollars.
choices.C: x*=6, y*=7.5 because interior tangency requires spending half on each good.
choices.D: x*=8, y*=5 because MRS equals p_x/p_y at that bundle.

questions.explanation: For perfect substitutes, compare utility per dollar. x gives 3/5=0.6 utility per dollar; y gives 1/4=0.25. The consumer buys only x, so x=60/5=12. B uses price alone. C incorrectly imposes an interior split. D invents tangency for linear preferences where MRS=3 does not equal p_x/p_y=1.25.

questions.question: Bianca has utility U(x,y)=2x+5y, income m=100, p_x=4, and p_y=20. Which bundle maximizes utility, and what does it show about corner solutions?

choices.A: x*=25, y*=0 because x gives more units per dollar.
choices.B: x*=0, y*=5 because y has the larger marginal utility.
choices.C: x*=25, y*=0 because MU_x/p_x=0.5 exceeds MU_y/p_y=0.25.
choices.D: x*=12.5, y*=2.5 because both goods must be consumed when utility is increasing.

questions.explanation: Utility per dollar is 2/4=0.5 for x and 5/20=0.25 for y, so all income is spent on x: x=25. A gives the right bundle but incomplete and potentially price-only reasoning. B uses marginal utility without prices. D wrongly assumes monotonicity implies an interior solution.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=4x+y, m=80, p_x=8, and p_y=2, the consumer initially buys only x or only y? If p_x rises to 12, what changes and why?

choices.A: Initially buys x; after p_x rises, switches to y because x's utility per dollar falls from 0.5 to 0.333 while y remains 0.5.
choices.B: Initially buys y; after p_x rises, switches to x because x becomes scarce.
choices.C: Initially buys both; after p_x rises, buys less x and more y by tangency.
choices.D: Initially buys x; after p_x rises, still buys x because MU_x is unchanged.

questions.explanation: Initially MU_x/p_x=4/8=0.5 and MU_y/p_y=1/2=0.5, so any budget-line bundle is optimal. After p_x rises, x gives 4/12=0.333 while y gives 0.5, so the optimum becomes all y. A captures the switch mechanism though the initial equal-return case means buying x is one optimal choice. B reverses the comparison. C wrongly applies tangency. D ignores price per utility.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=x+3y, m=90, p_x=3, p_y=12. If p_y falls to 6, what happens to the optimal corner and what is the mechanism?

choices.A: The optimum switches from all x to all y because y's utility per dollar rises from 3/12=0.25 to 3/6=0.5, exceeding x's 1/3.
choices.B: The optimum stays all x because x is still cheaper in dollars.
choices.C: The consumer buys both goods because the lower price creates tangency.
choices.D: The optimum switches from all y to all x because y becomes less scarce.

questions.explanation: Initially x gives 1/3 utility per dollar and y gives 1/4, so all x. After p_y falls to 6, y gives 1/2, so all y. The mechanism is a change in utility per dollar. B uses price alone. C incorrectly imposes tangency. D reverses the initial and final corners.

questions.question: A consumer has linear indifference curves for train rides x and bus rides y with slope -2. The budget line has slope -1. On a graph, where is the optimum and why?

choices.A: At the y-intercept because the budget line is flatter than the indifference curves.
choices.B: At the x-intercept because the indifference curves are steeper than the budget line, so x has higher value relative to its market cost.
choices.C: At an interior tangency because straight lines always cross once.
choices.D: At every point on the budget line because both curves are downward sloping.

questions.explanation: Linear indifference curves with slope -2 imply MRS=2, while budget slope -1 implies p_x/p_y=1. Since MRS exceeds the market trade-off, the consumer chooses the x-intercept. A reverses direction. C ignores lack of tangency. D would require identical slopes.

questions.question: For goods x and y, a consumer's indifference curves are straight lines with slope -1/2, while the budget line has slope -2. Which graphical conclusion is correct?

choices.A: The optimum is at the x-intercept because the budget line is steeper.
choices.B: The optimum is at an interior point because the curves cross.
choices.C: The optimum is at the y-intercept because the consumer's MRS is below the market trade-off.
choices.D: All points on the budget line are optimal because both slopes are negative.

questions.explanation: MRS=1/2 and p_x/p_y=2. The consumer is willing to give up only 1/2 y for x, but the market requires 2 y, so x is relatively unattractive and the y-intercept is optimal. A reverses the conclusion. B ignores the linear corner result. D requires equal slopes.

questions.question: Why can the tangency condition fail to characterize the optimum for perfect substitutes such as U(x,y)=2x+y?

choices.A: Because marginal utilities do not exist for linear utility.
choices.B: Because the MRS is constant, so unless it exactly equals the price ratio, the best affordable bundle lies at an endpoint of the budget line.
choices.C: Because consumers with linear utility do not face budget constraints.
choices.D: Because utility is decreasing in one of the goods.

questions.explanation: With perfect substitutes, MRS is constant. If it differs from the price ratio, one good gives higher utility per dollar at every bundle, producing a corner solution. A is wrong because marginal utilities do exist. C ignores the consumer problem. D is false for positive coefficients.

questions.question: A commuter views Lyft rides x and taxi rides y as perfect substitutes: each ride gets her to work equally well, so U(x,y)=x+y. A Lyft ride costs $10, a taxi ride costs $15, and her monthly ride budget is $300. Which model prediction is correct?

choices.A: She buys 15 Lyft rides and 10 taxi rides because variety is preferred.
choices.B: She buys 0 Lyft rides and 20 taxi rides because taxis are more expensive and therefore higher quality.
choices.C: She buys 30 Lyft rides and 0 taxi rides because Lyft gives the same marginal utility at a lower price.
choices.D: She buys any combination along the budget line because the services are substitutes.

questions.explanation: For perfect substitutes with equal marginal utilities, the cheaper good gives greater utility per dollar. Lyft yields 1/10 versus taxi 1/15, so all spending goes to Lyft. A assumes variety without convexity. B confuses price with utility. D would be true only if prices were equal.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes a corner solution from a boundary point that is not optimal?

choices.A: A corner solution is a boundary bundle that maximizes utility; a nonoptimal boundary point lies on the boundary but has another affordable bundle with higher utility.
choices.B: Every boundary point is a corner solution.
choices.C: A corner solution violates the budget constraint, while a nonoptimal boundary point satisfies it.
choices.D: A corner solution occurs only with perfect complements, while nonoptimal boundary points occur only with Cobb-Douglas utility.

questions.explanation: A corner solution is an optimum at the boundary. Not every boundary point is optimal. B is the common confusion. C is wrong because optima must be feasible. D assigns corner solutions to the wrong preference type.

questions.question: A consumer has U(x,y)=2x+y and income m=40. Initially p_x=4 and p_y=4. Then p_x falls to 2 while p_y stays 4. What is the change in maximized utility?

choices.A: Utility rises from 20 to 40 because the consumer buys more x at the cheaper price.
choices.B: Utility rises from 10 to 20 because the quantity of x doubles.
choices.C: Utility is unchanged at 10 because income is unchanged.
choices.D: Utility falls from 40 to 20 because lower price lowers total spending.

questions.explanation: Initially x gives 2/4=0.5 utility per dollar and y gives 1/4=0.25, so buy x=10 and U=20. After p_x=2, buy x=20 and U=40. B reports quantities as utility. C ignores the price-induced budget expansion. D reverses welfare effects.

questions.question: Carlos has U(x,y)=x^{2/5}y^{3/5}, income m=250, p_x=10, and p_y=5. What is the utility-maximizing bundle, and how should the result be interpreted?

choices.A: x*=10, y*=30; the consumer spends 2/5 of income on x and 3/5 on y.
choices.B: x*=25, y*=0; the lower price of y is irrelevant because x has positive utility.
choices.C: x*=12.5, y*=25; the consumer spends equal amounts because both goods enter utility.
choices.D: x*=15, y*=20; the exponents determine quantities directly.

questions.explanation: Cobb-Douglas demands are x=(2/5)(250)/10=10 and y=(3/5)(250)/5=30. Exponents are expenditure shares when they sum to one. B is a wrong corner. C uses equal spending. D treats exponents as quantity shares.

questions.question: Dana maximizes U(x,y)=x^2y subject to 6x+3y=180. Which bundle solves the problem, and what expenditure shares does it imply?

choices.A: x*=15, y*=30; spending is split evenly.
choices.B: x*=20, y*=20; spending on x is twice spending on y.
choices.C: x*=30, y*=0; the exponent on x is larger, so y is not consumed.
choices.D: x*=10, y*=40; spending on y is twice spending on x.

questions.explanation: For U=x^2y, total exponent is 3. x gets 2/3 of income: x=(2/3)(180)/6=20. y gets 1/3: y=(1/3)(180)/3=20. Spending is 120 on x and 60 on y. A uses equal expenditure. C incorrectly creates a corner. D reverses shares.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=x^{1/3}y^{2/3}, p_x=4, p_y=8, and m=240. If income falls to 180, what happens to x* and why?

choices.A: x* falls from 20 to 15 because x receives a fixed one-third expenditure share and income falls.
choices.B: x* stays at 20 because Cobb-Douglas demand depends only on prices.
choices.C: x* rises from 20 to 30 because lower income raises marginal utility.
choices.D: x* falls from 30 to 22.5 because x receives two-thirds of income.

questions.explanation: x=(1/3)m/p_x. Initially x=(1/3)(240)/4=20; after income falls, x=(1/3)(180)/4=15. The mechanism is proportional income response under fixed expenditure shares. B describes quasilinear demand. C reverses the income effect. D uses the wrong exponent share.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=x^{3/4}y^{1/4}, m=160, p_x=4, and p_y=4. If p_x doubles to 8, what happens to x*?

choices.A: x* remains 30 because the expenditure share on x is unchanged.
choices.B: x* falls from 30 to 15 because the same three-fourths expenditure share buys half as many units.
choices.C: x* rises from 30 to 60 because x is more valuable after its price rises.
choices.D: x* falls from 10 to 5 because x receives only one-fourth of income.

questions.explanation: x=(3/4)m/p_x. Initially x=(3/4)(160)/4=30; after p_x=8, x=15. The mechanism is constant expenditure share divided by a higher price. A ignores quantity effects. C reverses price response. D uses y's exponent.

questions.question: For Cobb-Douglas preferences U(x,y)=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}, what graphical feature helps guarantee an interior solution when prices and income are positive?

choices.A: Indifference curves are L-shaped with kinks on a fixed-proportion ray.
choices.B: Indifference curves are straight lines, so the optimum is normally at an intercept.
choices.C: Indifference curves approach the axes without touching them and have diminishing MRS, so tangency occurs in the positive quadrant.
choices.D: Indifference curves are upward sloping, so more of both goods lowers utility.

questions.explanation: Cobb-Douglas preferences are smooth, monotonic, strictly convex, and have indifference curves asymptotic to the axes, producing positive demand for both goods. A describes perfect complements. B describes perfect substitutes. D contradicts monotonicity.

questions.question: A Cobb-Douglas consumer faces a budget line that shifts outward in parallel after income increases. With x on the horizontal axis, what happens to the optimal point graphically?

choices.A: It moves to a higher indifference curve along an income expansion path through the origin if preferences are homothetic.
choices.B: It stays on the same indifference curve because prices did not change.
choices.C: It moves to the x-intercept because more income eliminates the need for y.
choices.D: It moves to a lower indifference curve because the budget set expands.

questions.explanation: Cobb-Douglas preferences are homothetic, so income expansion paths are rays through the origin when prices are fixed. Higher income reaches a higher indifference curve. B ignores the expanded budget. C wrongly predicts a corner. D reverses welfare.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=x^ay^b with a,b>0, why does the consumer spend a constant share of income on each good?

choices.A: Because the marginal utility of each good is constant.
choices.B: Because the first-order conditions imply expenditure ratios proportional to the exponents: p_xx/p_yy=a/b.
choices.C: Because prices do not affect Cobb-Douglas demand.
choices.D: Because the consumer always buys equal quantities of both goods.

questions.explanation: The FOCs imply a y/(b x)=p_x/p_y, which rearranges to p_xx/p_yy=a/b. Thus expenditure shares are a/(a+b) and b/(a+b). A is false because marginal utilities diminish. C is false because prices affect quantities. D is true only in special cases.

questions.question: A grocery analyst estimates that households allocate a stable 30 percent of their snack budget to chips x and 70 percent to salsa y over a wide range of incomes and prices. Which utility model best matches this pattern, and what demand for chips follows when m=200 and p_x=4?

choices.A: Perfect substitutes; x*=50 because chips are bought exclusively.
choices.B: Perfect complements; x*=30 because quantities must be fixed.
choices.C: Cobb-Douglas U=x^{0.3}y^{0.7}; x*=15 because chips receive 30 percent of expenditure.
choices.D: Quasilinear utility; x*=30 because income does not matter.

questions.explanation: Constant expenditure shares are the signature of Cobb-Douglas demand. Chips demand is x=0.3(200)/4=15. A predicts corners. B predicts fixed proportions, not expenditure shares. D predicts income-independent x demand, not constant budget shares.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes Cobb-Douglas utility from perfect substitutes utility?

choices.A: Cobb-Douglas normally yields interior solutions with diminishing MRS; perfect substitutes have constant MRS and typically yield corner solutions.
choices.B: Cobb-Douglas has constant MRS; perfect substitutes have diminishing MRS.
choices.C: Cobb-Douglas always yields corner solutions; perfect substitutes always yield interior solutions.
choices.D: Both always generate the same demand functions when prices are positive.

questions.explanation: Cobb-Douglas has diminishing MRS and positive demand for both goods when exponents are positive. Perfect substitutes have linear indifference curves and constant MRS, giving corners except when slopes match. B reverses properties. C reverses typical outcomes. D ignores distinct preference structures.

questions.question: Eli has U(x,y)=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}, m=100, and initially p_x=p_y=5. Then p_x falls to 2 while p_y remains 5. What is the change in maximized utility?

choices.A: Utility rises from 10 to approximately 15.81 because the lower price of x expands feasible consumption.
choices.B: Utility rises from 20 to 50 because x demand rises.
choices.C: Utility stays at 10 because income and p_y are unchanged.
choices.D: Utility falls from 15.81 to 10 because the budget line rotates outward.

questions.explanation: Initially x=y=10 and U=10. After p_x=2, x=100/(22)=25 and y=100/(25)=10, so U=sqrt(250)=15.81. B confuses quantity with utility. C ignores the price fall. D reverses the direction of welfare change.

questions.question: A consumer has U(x,y)=5x+2y, income m=100, p_x=10, and p_y=4. What is the optimal bundle?

choices.A: x*=10, y*=0 because x has higher marginal utility.
choices.B: Any bundle on 10x+4y=100 is optimal because MU_x/p_x=MU_y/p_y=0.5.
choices.C: x*=0, y*=25 because y is cheaper.
choices.D: x*=5, y*=12.5 because interior consumption balances the two goods.

questions.explanation: Utility per dollar is 5/10=0.5 for x and 2/4=0.5 for y. When these are equal, every affordable bundle on the budget line gives the same utility. A uses marginal utility alone. C uses price alone. D wrongly imposes an interior balancing rule.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=2x+3y, p_x=8, p_y=6, and m=120, which bundle maximizes utility and why?

choices.A: x*=15, y*=0 because x has a lower price per unit.
choices.B: x*=7.5, y*=10 because both goods have positive marginal utility.
choices.C: x*=0, y*=20 because MU_y/p_y=0.5 exceeds MU_x/p_x=0.25.
choices.D: Any bundle on the budget line because linear utility always produces multiple optima.

questions.explanation: Compare utility per dollar: x gives 2/8=0.25, y gives 3/6=0.5. All income goes to y, so y=120/6=20. A uses price alone. B incorrectly assumes monotonicity implies positive quantities of both. D is true only when utility per dollar is equal.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=3x+y, m=90, p_x=9, p_y=3. If p_x falls to 6, what happens to the set of optimal bundles?

choices.A: It changes from all y to any bundle on the budget line because x and y then give equal utility per dollar.
choices.B: It changes from any bundle on the budget line to all x because x's utility per dollar rises above y's.
choices.C: It stays all y because y remains cheaper.
choices.D: It becomes an interior tangency with x=7.5 and y=15.

questions.explanation: Initially x gives 3/9=1/3 and y gives 1/3, so every budget-line bundle is optimal. After p_x=6, x gives 3/6=1/2, exceeding y's 1/3, so all x. A reverses the initial and final cases. C uses price alone. D imposes a nonexistent tangency.

questions.question: A consumer has U(x,y)=x+4y, income m=80, p_x=2, and p_y=16. If p_y falls to 8, how does the optimum change?

choices.A: It stays all x because x remains cheaper.
choices.B: It switches from all x to any bundle on the budget line because both goods then yield 0.5 utility per dollar.
choices.C: It switches from all y to all x because y becomes cheaper.
choices.D: It becomes x=20, y=5 because the goods are consumed in equal utility amounts.

questions.explanation: Initially x gives 1/2=0.5 and y gives 4/16=0.25, so all x. After p_y=8, y gives 4/8=0.5, equal to x. Any budget-line bundle is optimal. A ignores the equal utility per dollar after the price change. C reverses the starting point. D imposes an arbitrary split.

questions.question: A perfect-substitutes consumer has straight indifference curves with slope -4. The budget line has slope -2. What does the graph imply?

choices.A: The optimum is at the x-intercept because the indifference curves are steeper than the budget line.
choices.B: The optimum is at the y-intercept because x is too expensive.
choices.C: The optimum is an interior tangency because the slopes are both negative.
choices.D: Every point on the budget line is optimal because the curves are straight.

questions.explanation: Slope -4 means MRS=4; budget slope -2 means p_x/p_y=2. Since the consumer values x more than its market cost, the x-intercept is optimal. B reverses the implication. C ignores unequal slopes. D is true only when slopes match.

questions.question: Indifference curves for U(x,y)=2x+y are straight lines. Suppose the budget line has exactly the same slope as the indifference curves. Which graphical statement is correct?

choices.A: Only the midpoint of the budget line is optimal.
choices.B: Only the x-intercept is optimal.
choices.C: Every point on the budget line lies on the same attainable indifference curve and is optimal.
choices.D: No optimum exists because the lines are parallel.

questions.explanation: When the budget line and linear indifference curves have the same slope, the entire budget line coincides with one indifference curve level, so all affordable boundary bundles are optimal. A invents a midpoint rule. B ignores equal slopes. D ignores compact feasible sets and attainable optima.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=ax+by, what does comparing a/p_x with b/p_y tell us?

choices.A: It compares total utility from each good.
choices.B: It compares marginal utility per dollar and determines which good gives more utility for each dollar spent.
choices.C: It compares the physical quantities of x and y.
choices.D: It determines whether preferences are convex or concave.

questions.explanation: For perfect substitutes, each dollar spent on x gives a/p_x utility, and each dollar spent on y gives b/p_y utility. The higher value determines the corner. A confuses marginal and total utility. C ignores utility coefficients. D is not what the ratio measures.

questions.question: A warehouse manager treats two brands of identical packing tape as perfect substitutes. Brand x costs $3 per roll and brand y costs $5 per roll. Each roll provides the same service. With a $150 budget, what should the manager buy?

choices.A: 30 rolls of y because higher price signals higher value.
choices.B: 25 rolls of x and 15 rolls of y to diversify suppliers.
choices.C: 50 rolls of x and 0 rolls of y because x gives the same utility at a lower price.
choices.D: Any combination because the tapes are perfect substitutes.

questions.explanation: Identical service means U=x+y. x gives 1/3 service units per dollar, y gives 1/5, so all spending goes to x: 150/3=50. A confuses price and value. B adds diversification not in the model. D would require equal prices.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes the unequal-slope and equal-slope cases for perfect substitutes?

choices.A: Unequal slopes produce a unique corner optimum; equal slopes produce many optima along the budget line.
choices.B: Unequal slopes produce no optimum; equal slopes produce one interior optimum.
choices.C: Unequal slopes produce a Cobb-Douglas solution; equal slopes produce a Leontief solution.
choices.D: Both cases always produce the same unique corner.

questions.explanation: For perfect substitutes, if MRS differs from p_x/p_y, one good has higher utility per dollar and the optimum is a unique corner. If they are equal, all budget-line bundles are optimal. B, C, and D each confuse the perfect substitutes model with other models or wrong uniqueness claims.

questions.question: A consumer has U(x,y)=x+y and m=60. Initially p_x=3 and p_y=6. Then p_y falls to 3. What is the change in maximized utility?

choices.A: Utility rises from 10 to 20 because y becomes cheaper.
choices.B: Utility remains 20 because initially all x is optimal, and after the price fall any mix of x and y on the new equal-price budget line gives utility 20.
choices.C: Utility rises from 20 to 40 because both goods are now affordable.
choices.D: Utility falls from 20 to 10 because the consumer loses a unique optimum.

questions.explanation: Initially x gives 1/3 utility per dollar and y gives 1/6, so all x: x=20 and U=20. After p_y=3, both goods give 1/3 utility per dollar, so every budget-line bundle gives U=20. A uses y quantity only. C double-counts affordability. D confuses multiplicity with lower welfare.

questions.question: A student has U(x,y)=min(x,2y), where x is left shoes and y is pairs of right shoes, income m=90, p_x=3, and p_y=6. What bundle maximizes utility?

choices.A: x*=15, y*=7.5 because the fixed-proportion condition is x=2y and the budget is exhausted.
choices.B: x*=30, y*=0 because x is cheaper.
choices.C: x*=0, y*=15 because y raises the second argument.
choices.D: x*=10, y*=10 because perfect complements require equal quantities.

questions.explanation: At the kink, x=2y. Budget gives 3(2y)+6y=90, so 12y=90 and y=7.5, x=15. B and C waste one complementary input. D wrongly assumes equal quantities instead of equality of the two utility arguments.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=min(3x,y), p_x=4, p_y=2, and m=80, which bundle is optimal and why?

choices.A: x*=20, y*=0 because x has the coefficient 3.
choices.B: x*=0, y*=40 because y is cheaper.
choices.C: x*=8, y*=24 because the optimum satisfies 3x=y at the kink.
choices.D: x*=10, y*=20 because spending must be equal across goods.

questions.explanation: For Leontief utility, set 3x=y. Budget 4x+2(3x)=80 gives 10x=80, so x=8 and y=24. A and B choose wasteful corners. D uses equal spending, which is not the fixed-proportion condition.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=min(x,y), m=100, p_x=5, p_y=5. If p_x rises to 15, what happens to the optimal bundle?

choices.A: It changes from (10,10) to (5,5) because the kink condition x=y remains, but the higher price makes each matched pair more expensive.
choices.B: It changes from (10,10) to (0,20) because x is now too expensive.
choices.C: It changes from (10,10) to (20,0) because y is relatively cheaper.
choices.D: It stays (10,10) because perfect complements have no substitution effect.

questions.explanation: The fixed proportion x=y remains. Initially 10 pairs cost 100. After p_x=15, each pair costs 20, so x=y=5. B and C ignore complementarity. D confuses zero substitution away from the fixed ratio with no price effect on affordability.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=min(2x,y), m=120, p_x=4, p_y=4. If income rises to 180, what happens to x* and y*?

choices.A: They remain x=10, y=20 because prices are unchanged.
choices.B: They rise from x=10, y=20 to x=15, y=30 along the fixed-proportion ray y=2x.
choices.C: Only x rises because income effects apply only to the first good.
choices.D: They shift to a corner because higher income makes tangency possible.

questions.explanation: The kink condition is 2x=y. Initially 4x+4(2x)=120 gives x=10, y=20. After income 180, 12x=180 gives x=15, y=30. A ignores income. C violates fixed proportions. D wrongly applies tangency.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=min(x,y), indifference curves are L-shaped with kinks along the 45-degree line. Why is the optimum on the kink rather than on a vertical or horizontal segment?

choices.A: Away from the kink, one good is in excess and does not raise utility, so spending on the excess good is wasted.
choices.B: The kink is where the budget line is always tangent to a smooth curve.
choices.C: The vertical segment gives the highest x-intercept.
choices.D: The horizontal segment always has a higher slope than the budget line.

questions.explanation: For perfect complements, utility is limited by the smaller component. Off the kink, extra units of one good do not increase utility. B incorrectly claims smooth tangency. C and D do not explain the Leontief optimum.

questions.question: A consumer has U(x,y)=min(x,3y). On a graph with x horizontal and y vertical, along what ray do the kinks of the indifference curves lie?

choices.A: y=3x because y must be three times x.
choices.B: x=3y because the two arguments x and 3y must be equal.
choices.C: x=y because all perfect complements use equal quantities.
choices.D: y=-3x because indifference curves slope downward.

questions.explanation: At the kink, x=3y. A reverses the fixed-proportion condition. C ignores coefficients. D confuses the kink ray with a downward-sloping budget or indifference segment.

questions.question: Why does the tangency condition MRS=p_x/p_y generally fail for perfect complements?

choices.A: Because the optimum occurs at a kink where the indifference curve is not differentiable, so a single MRS is not defined.
choices.B: Because perfect complements have constant MRS everywhere.
choices.C: Because perfect complements are not subject to budget constraints.
choices.D: Because utility decreases when both goods increase.

questions.explanation: Leontief indifference curves are L-shaped and nondifferentiable at the kink, where the optimum occurs. B describes perfect substitutes. C is false. D violates monotonicity.

questions.question: A restaurant uses burger patties x and buns y in fixed one-to-one proportions. Extra patties without buns and extra buns without patties do not help. Patties cost $2, buns cost $1, and the budget is $90. Which consumer model and solution match this scenario?

choices.A: Perfect substitutes; buy only buns because they are cheaper.
choices.B: Cobb-Douglas; spend half the budget on each input.
choices.C: Perfect complements U=min(x,y); buy x=30 patties and y=30 buns.
choices.D: Quasilinear utility; buy x until MU_x=p_x.

questions.explanation: The one-to-one fixed use pattern is perfect complements. Set x=y and 2x+1y=90, so 3x=90 and x=y=30. A ignores complementarity. B assumes substitutability and smooth convexity. D is unrelated.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes perfect complements from Cobb-Douglas preferences?

choices.A: Perfect complements require fixed proportions and have kinked indifference curves; Cobb-Douglas allows smooth substitution and has interior tangencies.
choices.B: Perfect complements always produce corners; Cobb-Douglas always produces corners.
choices.C: Perfect complements have straight indifference curves; Cobb-Douglas has L-shaped curves.
choices.D: Perfect complements depend only on prices; Cobb-Douglas depends only on income.

questions.explanation: Perfect complements are used in fixed proportions and are represented by L-shaped curves. Cobb-Douglas has smooth, convex curves with diminishing MRS. B wrongly says complements produce corners. C reverses shapes. D misstates demand determinants.

questions.question: Nina has U(x,y)=min(x,y), m=120, and initially p_x=p_y=6. Then p_x falls to 2 while p_y remains 6. What is the change in maximized utility?

choices.A: Utility rises from 10 to 15 because each matched pair becomes cheaper.
choices.B: Utility rises from 20 to 60 because x becomes cheaper.
choices.C: Utility stays 10 because there is no substitution effect.
choices.D: Utility falls from 15 to 10 because lower p_x creates excess x.

questions.explanation: Initially x=y and each pair costs 12, so utility is 120/12=10. After p_x=2, each pair costs 8, so x=y=15 and utility is 15. B assumes all spending goes to x. C confuses zero substitution effect with no total price effect. D reverses welfare.

questions.question: A consumer has U(x,y)=10ln x+y, income m=100, p_x=2, and p_y=1. Assuming an interior solution for y, what is the optimal bundle?

choices.A: x*=5, y*=90 because MU_x/p_x=MU_y/p_y implies 10/x divided by 2 equals 1.
choices.B: x*=25, y*=50 because half of income is spent on x.
choices.C: x*=50, y*=0 because x has diminishing marginal utility.
choices.D: x*=10, y*=80 because MU_x=MU_y.

questions.explanation: MU_x=10/x and MU_y=1. Interior condition MU_x/p_x=1 gives (10/x)/2=1, so x=5. Budget gives y=100-2(5)=90. B imposes Cobb-Douglas shares. C ignores the interior FOC and y consumption. D equates marginal utilities instead of marginal utility per dollar.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=8sqrt(x)+y, p_x=2, p_y=1, and m=50, what is the optimal bundle if y is positive?

choices.A: x*=16, y*=18 because MU_x/p_x=MU_y/p_y gives 4/sqrt(x)=1.
choices.B: x*=8, y*=34 because MU_x=MU_y gives 4/sqrt(x)=1.
choices.C: x*=25, y*=0 because all income should go to x.
choices.D: x*=12.5, y*=25 because income is split equally.

questions.explanation: MU_x=4/sqrt(x). Since p_x=2 and p_y=1, MU_x/p_x=1 gives 2/sqrt(x)=1, so x=4, not 16. However checking the answer text reveals A's stated FOC is inconsistent. The correct calculation is x*=4 and y*=42. None of the listed choices is mathematically correct.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=12ln x+y, p_x=3, p_y=1, and income high enough for y>0. If income rises from 60 to 90, what happens to x*?

choices.A: x* rises from 10 to 15 because x is normal.
choices.B: x* stays at 4 because quasilinear demand for x is pinned down by MU_x=p_x when p_y=1.
choices.C: x* falls from 4 to 3 because marginal utility diminishes.
choices.D: x* rises from 4 to 6 because spending on x is a constant share of income.

questions.explanation: For quasilinear utility with p_y=1, interior x solves v'(x)=p_x. Here 12/x=3, so x=4 regardless of income as long as y remains positive. A imposes a normal-good income effect on x. C reverses income logic. D uses Cobb-Douglas shares.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=20sqrt(x)+y, p_y=1, income high enough for y>0. If p_x rises from 5 to 10, what happens to x*?

choices.A: x* rises from 4 to 16 because the good becomes more valuable.
choices.B: x* stays at 4 because income is unchanged.
choices.C: x* falls from 4 to 1 because v'(x)=10/sqrt(x) must equal the higher price.
choices.D: x* falls from 16 to 4 because spending on x remains fixed.

questions.explanation: v'(x)=10/sqrt(x). At p_x=5, x=4. At p_x=10, x=1. The mechanism is movement along the marginal willingness-to-pay curve. A reverses price response. B ignores price. D uses incorrect initial demand and a fixed-spending-share mechanism.

questions.question: For quasilinear utility U(x,y)=v(x)+y with diminishing v'(x), what happens graphically to the chosen x when the budget line shifts outward in parallel and y remains positive?

choices.A: The chosen x stays the same; the consumer moves vertically to a higher indifference curve with more y.
choices.B: The chosen x rises proportionally with income along a ray through the origin.
choices.C: The optimum moves to the x-intercept.
choices.D: The indifference curves become flatter because income changes preferences.

questions.explanation: With quasilinear utility, the interior x is determined by v'(x)=p_x/p_y, not income. More income increases y while leaving x unchanged. B describes homothetic Cobb-Douglas preferences. C is not implied when y remains positive. D incorrectly says income changes preferences.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=ln x+y, the marginal willingness to pay for x declines as x increases. On a graph, what does the inverse demand curve for x look like?

choices.A: It is upward sloping because higher x raises marginal utility.
choices.B: It is downward sloping because v'(x)=1/x declines as x increases.
choices.C: It is vertical because x does not depend on income.
choices.D: It is horizontal because y enters linearly.

questions.explanation: For quasilinear utility, inverse demand for x is p_x=v'(x)p_y. With v'(x)=1/x and p_y=1, marginal willingness to pay declines as x rises. A reverses diminishing marginal utility. C confuses income independence with price independence. D misinterprets linearity in y.

questions.question: What is the main economic meaning of quasilinear utility U(x,y)=v(x)+y when y is money or a composite good?

choices.A: The marginal utility of income is constant, so income changes affect y but not the interior demand for x.
choices.B: Both goods always receive constant expenditure shares.
choices.C: The consumer always buys x and y in fixed proportions.
choices.D: The consumer always spends all income on x.

questions.explanation: Because y enters linearly, MU_y is constant. Interior x demand is determined by v'(x)=p_x/p_y and does not vary with income. B describes Cobb-Douglas. C describes perfect complements. D is a corner claim not implied by quasilinearity.

questions.question: A museum estimates a visitor's utility from exhibit visits x and remaining money y as U(x,y)=30ln x+y. Tickets cost $5 each and y is dollars. The visitor has enough income to buy positive y. Which prediction is correct?

choices.A: The visitor buys x=6 exhibit visits because 30/x=5.
choices.B: The visitor spends half of income on exhibit visits.
choices.C: The visitor buys no exhibit visits because money enters utility linearly.
choices.D: The visitor buys exhibits and money in a fixed 30-to-1 ratio.

questions.explanation: With quasilinear utility and p_y=1, set v'(x)=p_x: 30/x=5, so x=6. B uses Cobb-Douglas logic. C ignores positive marginal value of x. D invents a fixed-proportion rule.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes quasilinear demand from Cobb-Douglas demand?

choices.A: In quasilinear utility, interior demand for x is income-independent; in Cobb-Douglas utility, demand for each good is proportional to income.
choices.B: In quasilinear utility, both goods have fixed expenditure shares; in Cobb-Douglas utility, x demand is income-independent.
choices.C: Both always imply fixed proportions between x and y.
choices.D: Quasilinear utility always creates corner solutions; Cobb-Douglas never has interior solutions.

questions.explanation: Quasilinear interior x demand depends on prices, not income. Cobb-Douglas demands are linear in income with constant expenditure shares. B reverses the models. C describes perfect complements. D is false for both models.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=20ln x+y, p_y=1, m=100, and initially p_x=5. Then p_x falls to 4. Assuming y remains positive, what is the change in consumer surplus for good x using the quasilinear inverse demand p=20/x?

choices.A: 20ln(5/4)-4 because quantity rises from 4 to 5.
choices.B: 20ln(5/4) because the area under inverse demand between x=4 and x=5 measures the welfare gain from the price fall net of extra expenditure.
choices.C: 1 because quantity rises by one unit.
choices.D: 5 because the price falls by one dollar over five units.

questions.explanation: Initial x=20/5=4 and final x=20/4=5. For quasilinear utility, the surplus gain from the price change is the change in net surplus: integral from 4 to 5 of 20/x dx minus the cost effect? More directly, consumer surplus at price p is 20ln(20/p)-p(20/p). The increase from p=5 to p=4 is [20ln5-20]-[20ln4-20]=20ln(5/4). A incorrectly subtracts extra expenditure again. C uses quantity change. D uses a rectangle rather than the demand curve.

questions.question: Derive Marshallian demand for x when U(x,y)=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}, p_x=4, p_y=2, and m=80. What is x*(p_x,p_y,m) at these values?

choices.A: x*=10 because x=m/(2p_x).
choices.B: x*=20 because x=m/(2p_y).
choices.C: x*=5 because x=p_y m/(2p_x^2).
choices.D: x*=40 because all income is spent on x.

questions.explanation: For equal Cobb-Douglas, Marshallian demand is x=m/(2p_x). With m=80 and p_x=4, x=10. B uses y's price. C invents a formula. D gives a corner, not Cobb-Douglas demand.

questions.question: For U(x,y)=x^{2/3}y^{1/3}, derive the Marshallian demands at p_x=6, p_y=3, and m=180.

choices.A: x*=20, y*=20 because spending on x is twice spending on y.
choices.B: x*=10, y*=40 because y is cheaper.
choices.C: x*=30, y*=0 because x has the larger exponent.
choices.D: x*=15, y*=30 because quantities follow the exponents directly.

questions.explanation: Cobb-Douglas Marshallian demands are x=(2/3)m/p_x=20 and y=(1/3)m/p_y=20. Spending is 120 on x and 60 on y. B overweights the cheaper good. C wrongly creates a corner. D uses exponent shares as quantity shares.

questions.question: A Marshallian demand function is x*(p_x,p_y,m)=m/(4p_x). If all prices and income double from (p_x,p_y,m)=(5,10,100) to (10,20,200), what happens to x* and why?

choices.A: x* doubles because income doubles.
choices.B: x* is unchanged at 5 because Marshallian demand is homogeneous of degree zero in all prices and income.
choices.C: x* falls from 5 to 2.5 because p_x doubles.
choices.D: x* rises from 5 to 10 because p_y doubles.

questions.explanation: Marshallian demand is homogeneous of degree zero: scaling all prices and income by the same factor leaves real purchasing power unchanged. x=100/(45)=5 and 200/(410)=5. A ignores price scaling. C ignores income scaling. D incorrectly makes x depend positively on p_y here.

questions.question: For Cobb-Douglas demand x*=0.4m/p_x and y*=0.6m/p_y, initially m=200, p_x=4, p_y=6. If only p_x rises to 8, what happens to x*?

choices.A: x* rises from 20 to 40 because x becomes more expensive.
choices.B: x* remains 20 because the expenditure share is fixed.
choices.C: x* falls from 20 to 10 because the fixed 40 percent expenditure share buys fewer units.
choices.D: x* falls from 30 to 15 because x receives 60 percent of income.

questions.explanation: Initial x=0.4(200)/4=20. After p_x=8, x=0.4(200)/8=10. The mechanism is a higher own price reducing quantity for a fixed spending share. A reverses demand. B confuses fixed expenditure share with fixed quantity. D uses y's share.

questions.question: A Marshallian demand curve for x is drawn with p_x on the vertical axis and x on the horizontal axis, holding p_y and m fixed. For x*=60/p_x, what is the curve's shape?

choices.A: Downward sloping and convex to the origin because p_x=60/x.
choices.B: Upward sloping because higher prices imply higher spending.
choices.C: Horizontal because income is fixed.
choices.D: Vertical because p_y is held fixed.

questions.explanation: Demand x=60/p_x implies inverse demand p_x=60/x, a downward-sloping convex curve. B reverses the law of demand. C and D confuse holding variables fixed with the shape of the relationship between p_x and x.

questions.question: If income rises while p_x and p_y remain fixed, how is the Marshallian demand curve for a normal good x represented graphically?

choices.A: The demand curve shifts right because at each p_x the consumer demands more x.
choices.B: The demand curve shifts left because higher income raises opportunity cost.
choices.C: There is movement upward along the same demand curve only.
choices.D: The budget line becomes steeper with no demand shift.

questions.explanation: For a normal good, higher income increases quantity demanded at every own price, shifting Marshallian demand right. B describes an inferior-good response. C would describe a price change, not income change. D is false because fixed prices mean budget slope is unchanged.

questions.question: What does it mean that Marshallian demand satisfies the budget constraint when preferences are monotonic?

choices.A: The chosen bundle exhausts income: p_xx*(p_x,p_y,m)+p_yy*(p_x,p_y,m)=m.
choices.B: The chosen bundle always has x*=y*.
choices.C: The chosen bundle minimizes expenditure for every possible utility level.
choices.D: The chosen bundle does not depend on income.

questions.explanation: With monotonic preferences, more is better, so the consumer spends all income at the Marshallian optimum. B is not generally true. C describes Hicksian demand. D is false for most Marshallian demands.

questions.question: A business wants to forecast how many coffees x a student buys as coffee prices, snack prices, and weekly income vary. The student's preferences are U(x,y)=x^{1/4}y^{3/4}. Which object should the business estimate?

choices.A: A production function for coffee shops.
choices.B: A Marshallian demand function x*(p_x,p_y,m)=0.25m/p_x.
choices.C: A cost-minimization function holding utility fixed.
choices.D: A supply curve for snacks.

questions.explanation: The business wants ordinary demand as a function of prices and income, so Marshallian demand is appropriate. For Cobb-Douglas, x=0.25m/p_x. A and D are producer-side objects. C is Hicksian demand, not ordinary demand.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes Marshallian demand from Hicksian demand?

choices.A: Marshallian demand comes from utility maximization given income; Hicksian demand comes from expenditure minimization given a target utility.
choices.B: Marshallian demand holds utility fixed; Hicksian demand holds income fixed.
choices.C: Marshallian demand is only for firms; Hicksian demand is only for consumers.
choices.D: Marshallian demand ignores prices; Hicksian demand ignores utility.

questions.explanation: Marshallian demand solves the primal consumer problem with prices and income. Hicksian demand solves the dual problem with prices and utility. B reverses the definitions. C is false. D misstates both concepts.

questions.question: For quasilinear preferences, the Marshallian demand for x is x(p)=20-p for 0<=p<=20. If price falls from 12 to 8, what is the increase in consumer surplus?

choices.A: 32, the area under demand between prices 12 and 8: average quantity (8+12)/2 times price change 4.
choices.B: 16, because price falls by 4 and initial quantity is 8.
choices.C: 48, because price falls by 4 and final quantity is 12.
choices.D: 4, because the price falls by 4.

questions.explanation: At p=12, quantity is 8; at p=8, quantity is 12. The surplus gain is a trapezoid: ((8+12)/2)4=40, not 32. None of the answer choices gives the correct value; A uses the right method but incorrect arithmetic.

questions.question: Suppose Jordan's preferences over groceries x and other goods y are U(x,y)=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}, p_x=4, p_y=2, and income m=80. Find x, then determine whether x is normal using the demand function.

choices.A: x*=10, and x is normal because x*(m)=m/(2p_x) increases with income.
choices.B: x*=20, and x is inferior because y is cheaper.
choices.C: x*=10, and x is inferior because marginal utility of x diminishes.
choices.D: x*=0, and x is normal because income is positive.

questions.explanation: For Cobb-Douglas, x=m/(2p_x)=80/8=10. Since dx/dm=1/(2p_x)>0, x is normal. B uses y's price and wrong classification. C confuses diminishing marginal utility with inferiority. D wrongly creates a corner.

questions.question: A consumer has Marshallian demand for bus rides x given by x*(p_x,p_y,m)=30-0.1m when p_x=2 and p_y=1 over the relevant income range. If income is m=100, what is x*, and how is x classified?

choices.A: x*=20, and x is inferior because dx*/dm=-0.1<0.
choices.B: x*=20, and x is normal because demand is positive.
choices.C: x*=40, and x is inferior because demand rises with income.
choices.D: x*=10, and x is normal because income is high.

questions.explanation: At m=100, x=30-10=20. Since the income derivative is negative, x is inferior over this range. B confuses positive quantity with normality. C calculates incorrectly and misstates inferiority. D is arbitrary.

questions.question: For a normal good x, a student's income rises from $100 to $150 while prices remain fixed. Which statement best describes the comparative static effect?

choices.A: x demand decreases because higher income reduces marginal utility.
choices.B: x demand increases because the income effect is positive: dx*/dm>0.
choices.C: x demand is unchanged because prices did not change.
choices.D: x demand must double because income rose by 50 percent.

questions.explanation: A normal good is defined by dx*/dm>0, so a rise in income raises demand holding prices fixed. A confuses diminishing marginal utility with the income effect. C ignores income effects. D assumes unit income elasticity, which is not part of the definition.

questions.question: A demand function for instant noodles x is x*(m)=40-0.2m over the relevant income range. If income rises from 50 to 100, what happens to demand and why?

choices.A: Demand rises from 30 to 40 because x is normal.
choices.B: Demand falls from 30 to 20 because x is inferior over this range.
choices.C: Demand stays at 30 because prices are not changing.
choices.D: Demand falls from 40 to 20 because the price must have risen.

questions.explanation: x(50)=40-10=30 and x(100)=40-20=20. Demand falls as income rises, so the income effect is negative and the good is inferior. A reverses the sign. C ignores income. D invents a price change.

questions.question: On an Engel curve with income m on the vertical axis and quantity x on the horizontal axis, how would a normal good appear?

choices.A: As a curve where higher income is associated with higher quantity demanded, so the curve slopes upward when quantity is plotted horizontally.
choices.B: As a vertical line because normal goods do not respond to income.
choices.C: As a downward-sloping curve because higher income always reduces marginal utility.
choices.D: As a horizontal line because price is held fixed.

questions.explanation: An Engel curve relates income and quantity demanded holding prices fixed. For a normal good, quantity rises with income. B describes zero income effect. C describes an inferior good. D confuses an Engel curve with a price line.

questions.question: A budget line shifts outward in parallel when income rises. For an inferior good x on the horizontal axis, what happens to the chosen bundle along the income expansion path?

choices.A: The chosen x-coordinate decreases even though the budget set expands.
choices.B: The chosen x-coordinate must increase because the consumer can afford more.
choices.C: The budget line becomes steeper, causing substitution away from x.
choices.D: The optimum must move to the x-intercept.

questions.explanation: For an inferior good, higher income lowers demand for x, so the x-coordinate falls as the budget line shifts outward. B states the normal-good case. C is wrong because an income change does not change slope. D is not implied by inferiority.

questions.question: What does it mean formally for ramen x to be an inferior good at a particular price-income region?

choices.A: Its price is lower than the price of other foods.
choices.B: Its marginal utility is negative.
choices.C: Its Marshallian demand decreases as income rises, so partial derivative x*/partial m<0 holding prices fixed.
choices.D: Consumers buy zero units of it at all income levels.

questions.explanation: Inferiority is defined by a negative income derivative of Marshallian demand, holding prices fixed. A confuses cheapness with inferiority. B is not required; inferior goods can have positive marginal utility. D is not part of the definition.

questions.question: After receiving higher financial aid, a student buys fewer dollar-menu meals and more fresh groceries, while prices are unchanged. Which model classification best fits dollar-menu meals?

choices.A: They are normal goods because the student still buys some.
choices.B: They are inferior goods because demand falls when income rises.
choices.C: They are perfect complements because groceries increased.
choices.D: They are Giffen goods because all inferior goods are Giffen.

questions.explanation: Demand for dollar-menu meals decreases when income rises with prices fixed, so they are inferior over this range. A confuses positive consumption with normality. C is unrelated to fixed proportions. D confuses inferior goods with the special Giffen case.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes an inferior good from a Giffen good?

choices.A: An inferior good has negative income effect; a Giffen good is an inferior good whose negative income effect is strong enough to make quantity demanded rise when its own price rises.
choices.B: All inferior goods are Giffen goods.
choices.C: A Giffen good has positive income effect; an inferior good has positive substitution effect.
choices.D: Inferior goods violate utility maximization; Giffen goods violate the budget constraint.

questions.explanation: Inferiority concerns response to income. Giffen behavior concerns response to own price and requires a strong negative income effect overpowering substitution. B is the common overgeneralization. C misstates both effects. D is false.

questions.question: A student's Marshallian demand for campus meals is x*(m)=0.05m when meals are normal, and utility from meals in this reduced setting is measured as U=x. If income rises from 200 to 300, what is the change in this measured welfare from meals?

choices.A: 5, because demand rises from 10 to 15.
choices.B: -5, because meals are inferior.
choices.C: 100, because income rises by 100.
choices.D: 0, because prices do not change.

questions.explanation: x(200)=10 and x(300)=15. With U=x in this reduced measure, welfare from meals rises by 5. B contradicts the positive income derivative. C confuses income dollars with utility. D ignores the income effect. Welfare analysis is limited here because normal/inferior classification usually concerns demand responses, but this question uses a simple utility measure to make the welfare calculation directly applicable.
